food_subgroup: Stir-fried squid / cephalopod dishes with chilli-based sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried sambal squid; closest parent 'squid food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Squids and cuttlefishes) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for sambal-style stir-fried squid preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for stir-fried sambal squid.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried sambal squid is approximately 250 g, comprising squid pieces coated in sambal sauce, suitable for sharing as part of a multi-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: Sotong masak sambal (Squid cooked in sambal) — HPB Singapore Foodo / AFCD composite reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for squid baseline
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Sotong masak sambal' as the closest Singaporean equivalent. Macronutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo data for sambal squid preparations, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked squid (NDB 15150) and adjusted for sambal sauce contribution (oil, chilli, shrimp paste, sugar). Values represent a composite estimate for a typical stir-fried sambal squid dish as served in Singapore seafood restaurants.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; squid and seafood analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried sambal squid. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of plain cooked seafood/squid as very low GI (GI < 20), consistent with high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dishes. The sambal sauce contributes a small amount of sugar and chilli but does not materially raise GI above the low category. GL per 250 g serving = (15 × 4.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 1.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal paste, shrimp paste (belacan), and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation, as a 250 g serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (~4.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried sambal squid has a very low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice in large quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content from squid (~185 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium from sambal sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While squid is relatively low in saturated fat, its high dietary cholesterol content and the sodium-rich sambal sauce warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion control and reduced-oil preparation are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Sambal Squid (参巴炒鱿鱼; Sotong Goreng Sambal) is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian seafood dish in which fresh squid is wok-fried at high heat in a bold, aromatic sambal paste made from dried chillies, shrimp paste (belacan), onion, garlic, and a touch of sugar and lime juice. The dish is a staple of Chinese-Singaporean seafood restaurants and hawker centres, often served as part of a shared zi char meal. The squid is typically scored or cut into rings and tubes, then tossed rapidly in the wok to achieve a slightly charred, tender texture while absorbing the spicy, umami-rich sambal sauce. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, moderately to strongly spicy, with a subtle sweetness and a characteristic fermented depth from the belacan. Nutritionally, stir-fried sambal squid is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 118 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 5.2 g fat, and only 4.8 g carbohydrate, with a very low glycemic index of approximately 15. It is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and zinc. The sodium content is elevated due to the sambal paste and shrimp paste, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The cholesterol content from squid is notable but saturated fat is low. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and low carbohydrate load, but sodium and cholesterol management are important considerations for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried sambal squid?
Is sambal squid good for diabetics?
Is sambal squid high in sodium?
Can I eat sambal squid if I have high blood pressure?
Is stir-fried squid high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Stir-Fried Sambal Squid' was found; values are adapted from the closest HPB Foodo equivalent (sotong masak sambal) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy cooked squid data. The adaptation is well-supported and internally consistent, but the absence of an exact source match limits confidence to medium.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source references used. 3. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as squid and vegetable-oil-based sambal paste do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy squid data. 4. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Oils, seasonings and condiments', which is incorrect for a seafood dish; corrected to 'Fish and seafood' per Veritas content rules. 5. Cuisine tags in the input included 'Crab' and 'Lobster', which are not relevant to this dish; these have been disregarded. 6. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous seafood classification from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for sambal squid exists in the literature. 7. Halal status is marked false because traditional sambal squid preparations use belacan (shrimp paste), which may not be certified halal; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 8. The emoji in the original food_name ('Stir Fried Sambal Squid 👍') has been removed to produce a canonical English food name.
